line:4
Important: This section contains information that is specific to the new version of the driver framework (DFv2).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:6
Drivers provide software interfaces for communicating with hardware (or virtual) devices that are embedded in or connected to a system.
"Drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:7
In Fuchsia, drivers are user-space [components][components].
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:8
Like any other Fuchsia component, a driver is software that exposes and receives FIDL capabilities to and from other components in the system.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:11
Using these FIDL calls, Fuchsia components interact with drivers, which are bound to specific devices in the system.
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:14
Similar to Fuchsia’s component framework, which manages Fuchsia components, the [driver framework][driver-framework] manages the lifecycle and topology of all devices (known as [nodes][nodes]) and drivers in a Fuchsia system.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:14
Similar to Fuchsia’s component framework, which manages Fuchsia components, the [driver framework][driver-framework] manages the lifecycle and topology of all devices (known as [nodes][nodes]) and drivers in a Fuchsia system.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:15
Similar to Fuchsia’s component framework, which manages Fuchsia components, the [driver framework][driver-framework] manages the lifecycle and topology of all devices (known as [nodes][nodes]) and drivers in a Fuchsia system.
"nodes" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:15
Similar to Fuchsia’s component framework, which manages Fuchsia components, the [driver framework][driver-framework] manages the lifecycle and topology of all devices (known as [nodes][nodes]) and drivers in a Fuchsia system.
"nodes" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:15
Similar to Fuchsia’s component framework, which manages Fuchsia components, the [driver framework][driver-framework] manages the lifecycle and topology of all devices (known as [nodes][nodes]) and drivers in a Fuchsia system.
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)

line:4
This page provides a quick overview of the differences between the two versions of Fuchsia’s driver framework: [DFv1][dfv1] (legacy) and [DFv2][dfv2].
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:6
Key differences between DF1 and DFv2 are:
"Key" matches → …/NodePropertyKey a generic key (plain English)
line:15
Drivers are bound to devices and operate on them.
"Drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:16
Drivers can also create child devices.
"Drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:16
In DFv1, when a driver binds to a device, it must create a child device.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:17
The device is then owned by the driver that created it.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:20
In DFv2, we use the terms "[node][node]" and "node graph" (or "node topology").
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:20
In DFv2, we use the terms "[node][node]" and "node graph" (or "node topology").
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:20
In DFv2, we use the terms "[node][node]" and "node graph" (or "node topology").
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:20
In DFv2, we use the terms "[node][node]" and "node graph" (or "node topology").
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:21
A node can be thought of as something that exposes capabilities in a Fuchsia system.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:23
Drivers are bound to nodes and use their capabilities.
"Drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:23
Drivers are bound to nodes and use their capabilities.
"nodes" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:23
Drivers can also create child nodes.
"Drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:24
Drivers can also create child nodes.
"nodes" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:24
The node is then owned by the driver that is bound to it.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:24
The node is then owned by the driver that is bound to it.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:28
In DFv1, drivers are not components.
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:29
Drivers communicate with the driver framework using [`libDDK`][device-driver-lifecycle], which is a shared library that exposes functions.
"Drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:30
Drivers communicate with the driver framework using [`libDDK`][device-driver-lifecycle], which is a shared library that exposes functions.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:30
Drivers communicate with the driver framework using [`libDDK`][device-driver-lifecycle], which is a shared library that exposes functions.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:31
Drivers create a messageable device if they want to be placed in the `/dev` directory (a virtual file system).
"Drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:32
Then the driver framework will forward FIDL messages back to the driver.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:33
Then the driver framework will forward FIDL messages back to the driver.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:35
In DFv2, drivers are [components][components].
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:36
Some of these capabilities let them speak FIDL to the driver framework.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:37
Drivers can [advertise services][driver-services] to other drivers or non-driver components.
"Drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:37
Drivers can [advertise services][driver-services] to other drivers or non-driver components.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:37
Drivers can [advertise services][driver-services] to other drivers or non-driver components.
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:37
Drivers can [advertise services][driver-services] to other drivers or non-driver components.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:38
Using these services, drivers and other components can speak FIDL directly to each other.
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:43
In DFv1, drivers speak Banjo to each other.
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:44
To get the Banjo protocol, a driver requests it from its bound device.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:46
In DFv2, drivers speak [FIDL][fidl] to each other, like any other component in a Fuchsia system.
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:47
A driver gets a FIDL channel from its incoming component namespace.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)

line:11
This driver dispatcher supports the following features:
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:16
- Enable [driver transport FIDL][driver-transport] for in-process driver-to-driver communication.

- Enable [driver transport FIDL][driver-transport] for in-process driver-to-driver communication.

- Enable [driver transport FIDL][driver-transport] for in-process driver-to-driver communication.

line:17
- Enable [driver transport FIDL][driver-transport] for in-process driver-to-driver communication.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:23
A dispatcher, assigned to a driver, coordinates and performs asynchronous operations for the driver.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:24
A dispatcher, assigned to a driver, coordinates and performs asynchronous operations for the driver.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:24
All dispatchers in a driver host (therefore, in the same process) are backed by a pool of shared threads managed by the driver runtime.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:26
All dispatchers in a driver host (therefore, in the same process) are backed by a pool of shared threads managed by the driver runtime.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:26
Dispatchers can only operate within a driver runtime environment, such as a driver host or a driver testing framework.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:27
Dispatchers can only operate within a driver runtime environment, such as a driver host or a driver testing framework.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:27
Dispatchers can only operate within a driver runtime environment, such as a driver host or a driver testing framework.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:30
A dispatcher mainly handles two tasks: it schedules asynchronous work to run on threads in a driver host (in response to a callback created by a driver or a driver host) and it actually runs the work on those threads on behalf of a driver (or “calls into a driver”).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:30
A dispatcher mainly handles two tasks: it schedules asynchronous work to run on threads in a driver host (in response to a callback created by a driver or a driver host) and it actually runs the work on those threads on behalf of a driver (or “calls into a driver”).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:31
A dispatcher mainly handles two tasks: it schedules asynchronous work to run on threads in a driver host (in response to a callback created by a driver or a driver host) and it actually runs the work on those threads on behalf of a driver (or “calls into a driver”).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:32
A dispatcher mainly handles two tasks: it schedules asynchronous work to run on threads in a driver host (in response to a callback created by a driver or a driver host) and it actually runs the work on those threads on behalf of a driver (or “calls into a driver”).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:32
A dispatcher mainly handles two tasks: it schedules asynchronous work to run on threads in a driver host (in response to a callback created by a driver or a driver host) and it actually runs the work on those threads on behalf of a driver (or “calls into a driver”).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:34
A dispatcher may call into a driver in the following common scenarios:
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:36
- A driver hook is run (see [Default dispatcher](#default-dispatcher)).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:46
Dispatchers in a driver host may be created with different options to configure their own threading model for:
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:49
- Making parallel calls into a driver (see [Synchronized and unsynchronized](#synchronized-and-unsynchronized)) - Scheduling blocking operations (see [Synchronous operations](#synchronous-operations))
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:57
Created with the `FDF_DISPATCHER_OPTION_SYNCHRONIZED` option, synchronized dispatchers never make parallel calls into a driver.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:59
This may be thought of as a single-threaded dispatcher, with the caveat that it makes no guarantee that the dispatcher calls into a driver from the same thread each time (see [Thread local storage](#thread-local-storage)).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:63
Created with the `FDF_DISPATCHER_OPTION_UNSYNCHRONIZED` option, unsynchronized dispatchers may make parallel calls into a driver.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:64
However, with this option, there are no ordering guarantees; the driver is in charge of all synchronization of state.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:68
![Threading model](images/diagram-driver-dispatcher-01.svg "Diagram comparing callbacks scheduled by synchronized and unsynchronized dispatchers")
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:82
In general, drivers are discouraged from making synchronous calls because they can block other tasks from running.
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:83
However, if necessary, a driver can create a dispatcher with the `FDF_DISPATCHER_OPTION_ALLOW_SYNC_CALLS` option, which is only supported for [synchronized dispatchers](#synchronized-and-unsynchronized).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:89
This helps the driver runtime mitigate the chances that synchronous calls introduced by the driver would block other drivers in the same driver host.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:90
This helps the driver runtime mitigate the chances that synchronous calls introduced by the driver would block other drivers in the same driver host.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:90
This helps the driver runtime mitigate the chances that synchronous calls introduced by the driver would block other drivers in the same driver host.
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:90
This helps the driver runtime mitigate the chances that synchronous calls introduced by the driver would block other drivers in the same driver host.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:93
![Blocking tasks](images/diagram-driver-dispatcher-02.svg "Diagram comparing blocking and non-blocking tasks")
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:104
Thread 1 and 2 are shared by the dispatchers in a driver host to run both blocking and non-blocking tasks.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:106
However, the driver runtime is free to schedule asynchronous tasks on Thread 2 while Thread 1 is in use.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:111
A dispatcher may call into a driver from any shared thread managed by the driver runtime.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:111
A dispatcher may call into a driver from any shared thread managed by the driver runtime.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:112
However, the driver runtime makes no guarantee that it will be the same thread per call.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:113
For this reason, drivers should not use any thread-local storage (for instance, using the `thread_local` keyword in `C++`) since such storages tie the lifetime of variables to a distinct thread.
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:119
A dispatcher never makes reentrant calls into a driver – a call is considered reentrant if the driver has previously received a call from the dispatcher in the same call stack.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:120
A dispatcher never makes reentrant calls into a driver – a call is considered reentrant if the driver has previously received a call from the dispatcher in the same call stack.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:126
A driver host manages the lifetime of a driver's [default dispatcher](#default-dispatcher).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:126
A driver host manages the lifetime of a driver's [default dispatcher](#default-dispatcher).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:127
The driver host guarantees that this dispatcher is not destroyed until the driver is stopped.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:128
The driver host guarantees that this dispatcher is not destroyed until the driver is stopped.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:132
In DFv2, a dispatcher is provided to a driver as part of the driver’s start hook (that is, the `Start()` function in the driver’s code).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:132
In DFv2, a dispatcher is provided to a driver as part of the driver’s start hook (that is, the `Start()` function in the driver’s code).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:133
In DFv2, a dispatcher is provided to a driver as part of the driver’s start hook (that is, the `Start()` function in the driver’s code).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:134
This becomes the default dispatcher for the driver.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:134
To retrieve this dispatcher, the driver may call the `fdf::Dispatcher::GetCurrent()` function during a driver hook, such as `Start()`, `PrepareStop()`, and `Stop()`.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:135
To retrieve this dispatcher, the driver may call the `fdf::Dispatcher::GetCurrent()` function during a driver hook, such as `Start()`, `PrepareStop()`, and `Stop()`.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:138
In DFv1, a driver host creates a new dispatcher for a driver when it is bound.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:138
In DFv1, a driver host creates a new dispatcher for a driver when it is bound.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:139
To retrieve this dispatcher, the driver may call the `fdf::Dispatcher::GetCurrent()` function during a [driver hook][driver-hook] or [device hook][device-hook], such as `Bind()`, `Unbind()`, and `Release()`.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:140
To retrieve this dispatcher, the driver may call the `fdf::Dispatcher::GetCurrent()` function during a [driver hook][driver-hook] or [device hook][device-hook], such as `Bind()`, `Unbind()`, and `Release()`.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:140
To retrieve this dispatcher, the driver may call the `fdf::Dispatcher::GetCurrent()` function during a [driver hook][driver-hook] or [device hook][device-hook], such as `Bind()`, `Unbind()`, and `Release()`.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:145
In DFv2, the driver host automatically shuts down all dispatchers for a driver before the driver’s `Stop()` hook is called.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:145
In DFv2, the driver host automatically shuts down all dispatchers for a driver before the driver’s `Stop()` hook is called.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:146
In DFv2, the driver host automatically shuts down all dispatchers for a driver before the driver’s `Stop()` hook is called.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:146
If a driver wishes to be notified before the shutdown occurs, the driver may implement the `PrepareStop()` hook.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:147
If a driver wishes to be notified before the shutdown occurs, the driver may implement the `PrepareStop()` hook.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:149
In DFv1, a driver’s default dispatcher automatically shuts down after the `Unbind()` hook in the driver’s main device is called (which also causes all child devices to be unbound), but before the `Release()` hook is called – the driver's main device is the device added by the driver's `Bind()` hook.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:150
In DFv1, a driver’s default dispatcher automatically shuts down after the `Unbind()` hook in the driver’s main device is called (which also causes all child devices to be unbound), but before the `Release()` hook is called – the driver's main device is the device added by the driver's `Bind()` hook.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:152
In DFv1, a driver’s default dispatcher automatically shuts down after the `Unbind()` hook in the driver’s main device is called (which also causes all child devices to be unbound), but before the `Release()` hook is called – the driver's main device is the device added by the driver's `Bind()` hook.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:152
In DFv1, a driver’s default dispatcher automatically shuts down after the `Unbind()` hook in the driver’s main device is called (which also causes all child devices to be unbound), but before the `Release()` hook is called – the driver's main device is the device added by the driver's `Bind()` hook.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:153
The driver must handle the shutting down of any additional dispatchers it has created.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
Driver dispatcher performance
docs/concepts/drivers/driver-dispatcher-performance.md
line:9
This page identifies the [conditions](#conditions-non-inlined-calls) where calls cannot be made inlined, along with their performance implications.
resolves → …/Condition · first-use
line:9
This page identifies the [conditions](#conditions-non-inlined-calls) where calls cannot be made inlined, along with their performance implications.
resolves → …/Condition
line:45
A dispatcher does not make a call be inlined if any of the following conditions apply:
resolves → …/Condition
line:56
This condition only applies to [synchronized dispatchers][synchronized-dispatchers].
resolves → …/Condition
line:58
When calling into a driver, the driver runtime checks whether the dispatcher is idle.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:58
When calling into a driver, the driver runtime checks whether the dispatcher is idle.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:60
If it is not idle, a call is not made inlined to avoid making a parallel call into a driver.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:62
- **The call is from a thread that is not managed by the driver runtime**.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:64
A thread is not managed by the driver runtime when a driver has spawned their own thread using, for instance, the `std::thread` or `thread_create` method.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:64
A thread is not managed by the driver runtime when a driver has spawned their own thread using, for instance, the `std::thread` or `thread_create` method.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:71
FIDL transports send and receive messages using channels, either Zircon channels or driver transport channels.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:78
In Figure 3, when Driver A sends a FIDL request to Driver B, which is received inline, although Driver B immediately replies to Driver A, this message is considered [reentrant][reentrant] for Driver A.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:78
In Figure 3, when Driver A sends a FIDL request to Driver B, which is received inline, although Driver B immediately replies to Driver A, this message is considered [reentrant][reentrant] for Driver A.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:79
In Figure 3, when Driver A sends a FIDL request to Driver B, which is received inline, although Driver B immediately replies to Driver A, this message is considered [reentrant][reentrant] for Driver A.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:79
In Figure 3, when Driver A sends a FIDL request to Driver B, which is received inline, although Driver B immediately replies to Driver A, this message is considered [reentrant][reentrant] for Driver A.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:80
In Figure 3, when Driver A sends a FIDL request to Driver B, which is received inline, although Driver B immediately replies to Driver A, this message is considered [reentrant][reentrant] for Driver A.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:81
In this case, the dispatcher for Driver A makes a non-inlined call to send the reply from Driver B.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:81
In this case, the dispatcher for Driver A makes a non-inlined call to send the reply from Driver B.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:83
Reentrancy checks apply to dispatchers regardless of which drivers they serve.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:84
For example, if a driver owns two dispatchers, sending and receiving messages between these two dispatchers is always considered reentrant and cannot be inlined.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:90
FIDL inlining is supported only with the [driver transport][driver-transport].
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:90
FIDL inlining is supported only with the [driver transport][driver-transport].
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:91
Unlike Zircon channels, the driver transport channels are in-process only and they allow special inlining implementation in the driver transport channels logic.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:92
Unlike Zircon channels, the driver transport channels are in-process only and they allow special inlining implementation in the driver transport channels logic.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name

Driver binding
docs/concepts/drivers/driver_binding.md
line:15
To identify which drivers can bind to which nodes, each driver has bind rules and each node has a set of [node properties][node-properties].
resolves → …/BindRule · first-use
line:15
To identify which drivers can bind to which nodes, each driver has bind rules and each node has a set of [node properties][node-properties].
resolves → …/NodeProperties · first-use
line:16
A driver’s bind rules describe the qualification of a node that the driver can serve effectively.
resolves → …/BindRule
line:19
When the driver framework attempts to match a driver to a node, each unbound node’s properties are compared to the driver’s bind rules.
resolves → …/BindRule
line:19
If the node properties satisfy the driver’s bind rules, the driver framework allows the driver to bind to the node.
resolves → …/NodeProperties
line:19
If the node properties satisfy the driver’s bind rules, the driver framework allows the driver to bind to the node.
resolves → …/BindRule
line:24
When a Fuchsia system boots up, the driver manager tries to construct a node topology that represents all the hardware and virtual devices in the system, and the [driver index][driver-index] enumerates all the drivers known to the system.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:26
When a Fuchsia system boots up, the driver manager tries to construct a node topology that represents all the hardware and virtual devices in the system, and the [driver index][driver-index] enumerates all the drivers known to the system.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name

resolves → …/Driver · qualified name

line:33

line:38

line:41

resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:44
After the initial run of scanning and binding, whenever a new driver appears (for instance, a new driver is loaded to the system), the driver manager sends all unbound nodes in the topology to the driver index to be matched against the new driver.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name

When a node is matched, the driver manager binds this new driver to the node, an instance of the driver is placed in a driver host, and the driver host starts serving the device’s capabilities to other Fuchsia components in the system.

line:57
While drivers are often bound to devices, some drivers (such as PCI and ACPI) are bound to [boards](/docs/glossary/README.md#board) that may have multiple devices connected to them, both statically and dynamically.
resolves → …/Driver · link
line:57
While drivers are often bound to devices, some drivers (such as PCI and ACPI) are bound to [boards](/docs/glossary/README.md#board) that may have multiple devices connected to them, both statically and dynamically.
resolves → …/Driver · link
line:61
Upon the initial binding to a node, a [board driver](/docs/glossary/README.md#board-driver) (such as `acpi`) parses a binary blob passed from the system (which can be ACPI bytecode or a compiled device tree) and informs the driver manager of the static set of devices connected on the board.
resolves → …/Driver · link
line:61

resolves → …/Driver · link

Upon the initial binding to a node, a [board driver](/docs/glossary/README.md#board-driver) (such as `acpi`) parses a binary blob passed from the system (which can be ACPI bytecode or a compiled device tree) and informs the driver manager of the static set of devices connected on the board.
resolves → …/Driver · link
line:64
These devices get bound to drivers through the normal binding process orchestrated by the driver index.
resolves → …/Driver · link

When a Fuchsia system boots up, the driver manager tries to construct a node topology that represents all the hardware and virtual devices in the system, and the [driver index][driver-index] enumerates all the drivers known to the system.

Driver communication
docs/concepts/drivers/driver_communication.md
line:6
The component framework handles the routing of capabilities between components, which includes both drivers and non-drivers.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:6
The component framework handles the routing of capabilities between components, which includes both drivers and non-drivers.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:10
Drivers communicate with other drivers and non-drivers primarily using [service capabilities](/docs/concepts/components/v2/capabilities/service.md) (which this doc will refer to as "services" for brevity).
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:10
Drivers communicate with other drivers and non-drivers primarily using [service capabilities](/docs/concepts/components/v2/capabilities/service.md) (which this doc will refer to as "services" for brevity).
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:10
Drivers communicate with other drivers and non-drivers primarily using [service capabilities](/docs/concepts/components/v2/capabilities/service.md) (which this doc will refer to as "services" for brevity).
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:13
There are only two differences between service communication involving drivers and service communication between non-drivers, and it involves how services are routed.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:14
There are only two differences between service communication involving drivers and service communication between non-drivers, and it involves how services are routed.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:16
Driver-to-driver routing uses dynamic capability routing where the Driver Manager creates routes from a parent node to a child node during the child driver's component creation.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:16

resolves → …/Driver · qualified name

Services originating from drivers and passing to non-drivers are **Aggregated**, because all drivers reside in [collections](/docs/concepts/components/v2/realms.md#collections).

line:19

line:20

line:22
This means that all service instances exposed by drivers will be aggregated into the same directory named after the service name
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name

resolves → …/Driver · qualified name

resolves → …/Driver · qualified name

Driver framework
docs/concepts/drivers/driver_framework.md
line:4
Important: This page contains information that is specific to the new version of the driver framework (DFv2).
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:6
Fuchsia’s driver framework is a collection of libraries, tools, metadata, and components that enable developers to create, run, test, and distribute drivers for Fuchsia systems.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:7
Fuchsia’s driver framework is a collection of libraries, tools, metadata, and components that enable developers to create, run, test, and distribute drivers for Fuchsia systems.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:8
The driver framework aims to provide a stable ABI that allows developers to write a driver once and deploy it on multiple versions of the Fuchsia platform.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:9
The driver framework aims to provide a stable ABI that allows developers to write a driver once and deploy it on multiple versions of the Fuchsia platform.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:10
(However, Fuchsia's driver framework is constantly evolving and has not achieved ABI stability yet.)
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:13
The driver framework comprises the following entities for running drivers in a Fuchsia system:
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:13
The driver framework comprises the following entities for running drivers in a Fuchsia system:
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name

line:65
The driver index, which runs as a Fuchsia component (`driver-index.cm`), is responsible for the following tasks:
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:65
The driver index, which runs as a Fuchsia component (`driver-index.cm`), is responsible for the following tasks:
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:68
* Keep track of all the drivers available in the system, where each driver is stored along with metadata (such as its component URL and bind rules).
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:68
* Keep track of all the drivers available in the system, where each driver is stored along with metadata (such as its component URL and bind rules).
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:69
* Keep track of all the drivers available in the system, where each driver is stored along with metadata (such as its component URL and bind rules).
resolves → …/BindRule · first-use
line:70
* Compare all known drivers to a target [node][nodes] (that is, a device in the system) for matching.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:73
The driver index tracks the following types of drivers in a Fuchsia system:
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:73
The driver index tracks the following types of drivers in a Fuchsia system:
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:75
* **Boot drivers**: Drivers that exist in the Zircon boot image (ZBI) and are needed for bootstrapping the system (for example, storage drivers).
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:75
* **Boot drivers**: Drivers that exist in the Zircon boot image (ZBI) and are needed for bootstrapping the system (for example, storage drivers).
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:76
* **Boot drivers**: Drivers that exist in the Zircon boot image (ZBI) and are needed for bootstrapping the system (for example, storage drivers).
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:77
The ZBI has limited space compared to base drivers.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:78
* **Base drivers**: Drivers that are available in the Fuchsia image and are not critical for bootstrapping the system.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:78
* **Base drivers**: Drivers that are available in the Fuchsia image and are not critical for bootstrapping the system.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:79
These drivers are loaded from the system's storage, so they need to be loaded after the boot drivers have enabled storage (for example, USB drivers, networking drivers) These are similar to Fuchsia’s [base packages][base-packages].
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:80
These drivers are loaded from the system's storage, so they need to be loaded after the boot drivers have enabled storage (for example, USB drivers, networking drivers) These are similar to Fuchsia’s [base packages][base-packages].
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:81
These drivers are loaded from the system's storage, so they need to be loaded after the boot drivers have enabled storage (for example, USB drivers, networking drivers) These are similar to Fuchsia’s [base packages][base-packages].
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:81
These drivers are loaded from the system's storage, so they need to be loaded after the boot drivers have enabled storage (for example, USB drivers, networking drivers) These are similar to Fuchsia’s [base packages][base-packages].
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:83
* **Universe drivers**: Drivers that are registered manually after the system’s initial boot (for instance, using the `ffx driver register` or `bazel run` command), which are loaded using the universe package resolver, similar to Fuchsia’s [universe packages][universe-packages].
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:83
* **Universe drivers**: Drivers that are registered manually after the system’s initial boot (for instance, using the `ffx driver register` or `bazel run` command), which are loaded using the universe package resolver, similar to Fuchsia’s [universe packages][universe-packages].
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:84
* **Universe drivers**: Drivers that are registered manually after the system’s initial boot (for instance, using the `ffx driver register` or `bazel run` command), which are loaded using the universe package resolver, similar to Fuchsia’s [universe packages][universe-packages].
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:87
However, registering universe drivers is supported for driver development purposes only.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:87
However, registering universe drivers is supported for driver development purposes only.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:89
When the driver manager needs to find a driver for an unbound node in the [node topology][node-topology], it uses the [`MatchDriver`][fidl-matchdriver] FIDL protocol to send a match request to the driver index.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:89
When the driver manager needs to find a driver for an unbound node in the [node topology][node-topology], it uses the [`MatchDriver`][fidl-matchdriver] FIDL protocol to send a match request to the driver index.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:91
When the driver manager needs to find a driver for an unbound node in the [node topology][node-topology], it uses the [`MatchDriver`][fidl-matchdriver] FIDL protocol to send a match request to the driver index.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:91
The driver index then evaluates the [node properties][node-properties] (which is included in the match request) against the bind rules of every driver that is being tracked.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:92
The driver index then evaluates the [node properties][node-properties] (which is included in the match request) against the bind rules of every driver that is being tracked.
resolves → …/NodeProperties · first-use
line:93
The driver index then evaluates the [node properties][node-properties] (which is included in the match request) against the bind rules of every driver that is being tracked.
resolves → …/BindRule
line:93
The driver index then evaluates the [node properties][node-properties] (which is included in the match request) against the bind rules of every driver that is being tracked.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:94
When there is a match, the driver index returns the matched driver’s metadata to the driver manager.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:94
When there is a match, the driver index returns the matched driver’s metadata to the driver manager.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:95
When there is a match, the driver index returns the matched driver’s metadata to the driver manager.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:95
But if there is no match, the driver index responds with a `Not Found` error code.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:96
(For more information on the bind rules and binding process, see [Driver binding][driver-binding].)
resolves → …/BindRule
line:97
(For more information on the bind rules and binding process, see [Driver binding][driver-binding].)
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:97
(For more information on the bind rules and binding process, see [Driver binding][driver-binding].)
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:115
The FIDL interface in the driver framework refers to the following two FIDL protocols:
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:118
* [`fuchsia.driver.framework/Node`][fidl-node] * [`fuchsia.driver.framework/NodeController`][fidl-nodecontroller]
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:118
* [`fuchsia.driver.framework/Node`][fidl-node] * [`fuchsia.driver.framework/NodeController`][fidl-nodecontroller]
resolves → …/Node · qualified name
line:118
* [`fuchsia.driver.framework/Node`][fidl-node] * [`fuchsia.driver.framework/NodeController`][fidl-nodecontroller]
resolves → …/Node · qualified name
line:119
* [`fuchsia.driver.framework/Node`][fidl-node] * [`fuchsia.driver.framework/NodeController`][fidl-nodecontroller]
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:119
* [`fuchsia.driver.framework/Node`][fidl-node] * [`fuchsia.driver.framework/NodeController`][fidl-nodecontroller]
resolves → …/NodeController · first-use + qualified name
line:119
* [`fuchsia.driver.framework/Node`][fidl-node] * [`fuchsia.driver.framework/NodeController`][fidl-nodecontroller]
resolves → …/NodeController · qualified name
line:121
The combination of these two protocols forms the basis of how drivers communicate with the driver framework.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:122
The combination of these two protocols forms the basis of how drivers communicate with the driver framework.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:123
Other protocols supported by the framework are largely used for internal communication between various driver framework entities (that is, the driver manager, the driver index, the driver hosts, etc.).
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:124
Other protocols supported by the framework are largely used for internal communication between various driver framework entities (that is, the driver manager, the driver index, the driver hosts, etc.).
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:124
Other protocols supported by the framework are largely used for internal communication between various driver framework entities (that is, the driver manager, the driver index, the driver hosts, etc.).
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:124
Other protocols supported by the framework are largely used for internal communication between various driver framework entities (that is, the driver manager, the driver index, the driver hosts, etc.).
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:127
There are also some auxiliary FIDL protocols used between non-driver components and the driver framework.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:128
There are also some auxiliary FIDL protocols used between non-driver components and the driver framework.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:129
For instance, we have several protocols for debugging in the `fuchsia.driver.development` FIDL library which are used by our debugging tools.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:130
We also have some protocols for registering new drivers and handling shutdown.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:131
However, most of these are not particularly interesting for a driver developer.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name

Fuchsia's driver manager, which runs as a Fuchsia component (`driver_manager.cm`), is one of the initial processes to start when a Fuchsia system boots up.

line:25
Fuchsia's driver manager, which runs as a Fuchsia component (`driver_manager.cm`), is one of the initial processes to start when a Fuchsia system boots up.
"manager" matches → …/CompositeNodeManager a generic manager (plain English)
line:27
The driver manager starts and stops drivers in a Fuchsia system and routes FIDL capabilities for the drivers.

line:42

line:42
Every driver lives in a driver host, which runs as a Fuchsia component (`driver_host.cm`).

line:43
Driver hosts provide isolation between drivers in a Fuchsia system.

line:43
Driver hosts provide isolation between drivers in a Fuchsia system.

line:44
Each driver host is a process, meaning it has its own address space and manages its own set of threads.

line:49
The driver host then initializes the driver.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:49
A driver's initialization involves calling the driver's start hook (the `Start()` function in the driver’s code) and handing the driver control of the node to which it’s bound.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:49
A driver's initialization involves calling the driver's start hook (the `Start()` function in the driver’s code) and handing the driver control of the node to which it’s bound.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:50
A driver's initialization involves calling the driver's start hook (the `Start()` function in the driver’s code) and handing the driver control of the node to which it’s bound.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)

However, with the driver runtime, co-located drivers in the same process can avoid going in and out of the Zircon kernel.

line:104
However, with the driver runtime, co-located drivers in the same process can avoid going in and out of the Zircon kernel.
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:105
In other words, the driver runtime provides a mechanism that enables co-located drivers to communicate with each other locally, which is much more efficient than communicating using kernel channels.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:106
In other words, the driver runtime provides a mechanism that enables co-located drivers to communicate with each other locally, which is much more efficient than communicating using kernel channels.
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:108
The driver runtime is an in-process runtime modeled after the Zircon kernel.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:109
The driver runtime provides primitives similar to the Zircon channel and port, and a new FIDL transport is built on top of this runtime.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:111
(For more details, see the [RFC][rfc-driver-runtime] on the driver runtime.)

Drivers and nodes
docs/concepts/drivers/drivers_and_nodes.md
line:6
In Fuchsia, every driver is bound to a node.
resolves → …/Node · qualified name
line:6
A node is the main building block of the [driver framework][driver-framework].
resolves → …/Node · qualified name
line:7
A node can be thought of as a hardware or virtual device, or a node can be a part of a hardware device.
resolves → …/Node · qualified name
line:8
A node can be thought of as a hardware or virtual device, or a node can be a part of a hardware device.
resolves → …/Node · qualified name
line:9
For example, a GPIO node can represent a single GPIO pin attached to a GPIO controller while a RAM disk node can represent a virtual disk, not a real hardware device.
resolves → …/Node · qualified name
line:10
For example, a GPIO node can represent a single GPIO pin attached to a GPIO controller while a RAM disk node can represent a virtual disk, not a real hardware device.
resolves → …/Node · qualified name
line:13

resolves → …/Node · qualified name

line:107
For instance, a PCI driver’s component moniker may look like `/bootstrap/boot-drivers:root.sys.platform.pci.00_14_0`.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name
line:108

In the case of a composite node creation, the driver framework creates a composite node in response to a driver binding to multiple parent nodes, so it already knows the driver that will be bound to the node.
resolves → …/Driver · qualified name

line:4
Important: This page contains information that is specific to the new version of the driver framework (DFv2).
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:6
In Fuchsia, every driver is bound to a node.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:7
A node is the main building block of the [driver framework][driver-framework].
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:7
A node is the main building block of the [driver framework][driver-framework].
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:10
For example, a GPIO node can represent a single GPIO pin attached to a GPIO controller while a RAM disk node can represent a virtual disk, not a real hardware device.
"controller" matches → …/NodeController a generic controller (plain English)
line:13
Drivers, when bound to nodes, can create child nodes.
"Drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:17
![Node topology diagram showing a hierarchy from a root node to specific SPI device nodes, with drivers bound to nodes.](images/diagram_node_topology_01.svg "Basic node topology"){: width="550"}
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:20
A node topology where the green circles are nodes representing devices and the blue boxes are drivers.
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:25
A node has the following attributes:
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:27
* [Node properties][node-properties]: Key-value pairs that describe which driver can [bind][driver-binding] to the node.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:27
* [Node properties][node-properties]: Key-value pairs that describe which driver can [bind][driver-binding] to the node.

line:28
* [Node properties][node-properties]: Key-value pairs that describe which driver can [bind][driver-binding] to the node.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:29
The values can be of integer, string, boolean, or enum type.
"values" matches → …/NodePropertyValue a generic value (plain English)
line:31
* [Capabilities][capabilities]: FIDL capabilities that are offered to a driver when it is bound to the node.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:32
* [Capabilities][capabilities]: FIDL capabilities that are offered to a driver when it is bound to the node.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:34
* [Symbols][symbols]: Key-value pairs where the keys are strings and the values are virtual addresses.
"value" matches → …/NodePropertyValue a generic value (plain English)
line:35
* [Symbols][symbols]: Key-value pairs where the keys are strings and the values are virtual addresses.
"values" matches → …/NodePropertyValue a generic value (plain English)
line:35
A driver uses these symbols to hand pointers to a child driver for [in-process communication][driver-runtime].
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:36
A driver uses these symbols to hand pointers to a child driver for [in-process communication][driver-runtime].
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:36
A driver uses these symbols to hand pointers to a child driver for [in-process communication][driver-runtime].
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:37
The symbols are only provided to a driver if it is co-located in the same [driver host][driver-host] as its parent, otherwise the driver uses FIDL calls to communicate.

A node represents a collection of resources, which in turn represents a hardware or virtual device in a Fuchsia system.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:47
When a parent driver creates a new node, the parent driver specifies which capabilities are associated with that node.

line:49

line:53

line:54
However, not all capabilities in a driver’s incoming namespace are from a parent driver.

line:58
Fuchsia's [driver manager][driver-manager] maintains a node topology that describes the parent-child relationships between nodes (representing devices) in a Fuchsia system.
"manager" matches → …/CompositeNodeManager a generic manager (plain English)
line:58
Fuchsia's [driver manager][driver-manager] maintains a node topology that describes the parent-child relationships between nodes (representing devices) in a Fuchsia system.
"manager" matches → …/CompositeNodeManager a generic manager (plain English)
line:83
However, the node topology and the component topology are kept separate.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:84
The first reason is that not all nodes may have drivers bound to them.
"nodes" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:85
This means that these unbound nodes are not associated with any specific components shown in the component topology.
"nodes" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:86
In fact, many nodes in the node topology are commonly left unbound (that is, unmatched with drivers) while the system is running.
"nodes" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:86
In fact, many nodes in the node topology are commonly left unbound (that is, unmatched with drivers) while the system is running.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:88
Second, in the node topology a node may have multiple parent nodes (see [Composite nodes](#composite_nodes)), which isn’t allowed in the component topology.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:88

line:97
In the component topology, all driver components appear to be siblings of each other under their parent component, which is the driver manager.
"manager" matches → …/CompositeNodeManager a generic manager (plain English)
line:106
The driver framework names the moniker of a driver component based on the driver’s place in the node topology, not the component topology.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:108
This component moniker implies the following node topology for the PCI driver: `root` -> `sys` -> `platform` -> `pci` -> `00_14_0`.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:111
The component moniker shows that this PCI driver component is only 2 layers down in the component topology, but the node (`00_14_0`) bound to the PCI driver is 5 layers down in the node topology.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:112
The component moniker shows that this PCI driver component is only 2 layers down in the component topology, but the node (`00_14_0`) bound to the PCI driver is 5 layers down in the node topology.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:116
A driver’s lifetime in a Fuchsia system is tied to the lifetime of the node it’s bound to.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:119
A driver can perform the following lifecycle actions on a [`Node`][node-object] object it controls:
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:124
* [Drop the node](#node_removal), which causes the driver manager to clean up the node and its descendants in the node topology.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:124
* [Drop the node](#node_removal), which causes the driver manager to clean up the node and its descendants in the node topology.

When a driver creates a child node, the driver also has the ability to hold on to a [`NodeController`][node-controller] object of the child node.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:151
When a driver creates a child node, the driver also has the ability to hold on to a [`NodeController`][node-controller] object of the child node.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:151
The driver can use this object (by calling [`Remove`][fidl-remove]) to stop the child node at any time, which results in stopping the driver bound to the node.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:152
The driver can use this object (by calling [`Remove`][fidl-remove]) to stop the child node at any time, which results in stopping the driver bound to the node.

The driver can use this object (by calling [`Remove`][fidl-remove]) to stop the child node at any time, which results in stopping the driver bound to the node.
"node" matches → …/Node a generic node (plain English)
line:153
The driver receives the [`OnBind`][fidl-onbind] FIDL event through this object when the child node is bound to a driver.

line:161
* The driver bound to the node drops its `Node` object.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:164
* The component framework shuts down the driver component, therefore removes the driver bound to the node.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:165
* The component framework shuts down the driver component, therefore removes the driver bound to the node.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:168
If a node is bound to a driver, then the driver must be stopped before the node can be removed.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:168
If a node is bound to a driver, then the driver must be stopped before the node can be removed.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:169
And if the node to be removed is in the middle of the node topology, the driver framework ensures that all the child drivers are stopped and all the child nodes are removed before the target node is removed.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:170
And if the node to be removed is in the middle of the node topology, the driver framework ensures that all the child drivers are stopped and all the child nodes are removed before the target node is removed.
"drivers" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:175
The driver manager creates a composite node when a driver wishes to bind itself to multiple parent nodes.
"manager" matches → …/CompositeNodeManager a generic manager (plain English)
line:176
In this case, the driver manager adds the composite node to each parent node as a child node.
"manager" matches → …/CompositeNodeManager a generic manager (plain English)
line:177
The driver manager then binds the driver to the composite node.
"manager" matches → …/CompositeNodeManager a generic manager (plain English)
line:200
The driver manager creates a composite node named `camera` and adds the node as a child to both the `gpio-enable` node and the `pci-device` node.
"manager" matches → …/CompositeNodeManager a generic manager (plain English)
line:203
Lastly, the driver manager binds the `camera-driver` driver to the composite node.
"manager" matches → …/CompositeNodeManager a generic manager (plain English)
line:206
![Diagram showing a complex binding topology for a camera controller driver with multiple parent nodes.](images/diagram_composite_drivers_01.svg "Composite drivers example"){: width="550"}

line:21
In Fuchsia, a driver acquires the ability to read and write data in a device’s memory by obtaining a virtual memory object ([VMO][vmo]) that represents the device’s memory region in the system.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:27
When a driver reads from or writes to this region, it has the same effect as directly reading from or writing to the device’s registers.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:31
A root bus driver (such as the platform, PCI, or ACPI bus driver), which is aware of all devices connected to the bus, has a special capability to create VMOs for devices in the system.

line:31
A root bus driver (such as the platform, PCI, or ACPI bus driver), which is aware of all devices connected to the bus, has a special capability to create VMOs for devices in the system.
"driver" matches → …/Driver …/NodeController.WaitForDriver a generic driver (plain English)
line:33
Once created, these VMOs can be passed to other drivers.

line:34

Mechanical · deterministic · no model.  Counts, not percentages. Attribution is strongest for backticked FQNs and explicit links; plain-word matches carry their excerpt so you can judge.
